issues.title: API router pathMatches function is referenced but never defined
issues.description: The resolveRequiredScope function calls pathMatches() and normalize() but pathMatches is never defined in the file, causing a runtime error when any route resolution is attempted. The normalize helper is partially shown but pathMatches is completely missing.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/v1/router.ts, in the resolveRequiredScope function: add the missing pathMatches utility function. BEFORE: resolveRequiredScope calls pathMatches(r.path, normalize(path)) but pathMatches is not defined anywhere in the file, causing ReferenceError at runtime. AFTER: Add a pathMatches function before resolveRequiredScope that compares a route pattern (with :param segments) against a concrete path by splitting both on '/' and matching segment by segment, where ':'-prefixed segments match any value. Implementation: function pathMatches(pattern: string, concrete: string): boolean { const pParts = pattern.split('/').filter(Boolean); const cParts = concrete.split('/').filter(Boolean); if (pParts.length !== cParts.length) return false; return pParts.every((seg, i) => seg.startsWith(':') || seg === cParts[i]); }. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: App shell lacks ARIA landmarks and skip navigation for screen readers
issues.description: The main app layout in App.tsx renders sidebar and content area without proper ARIA landmark roles (navigation, main, complementary). The SkipToContent component is imported but the main content area lacks the corresponding id='main-content' target, making skip navigation non-functional.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, in the main layout JSX where the sidebar and page content are rendered: add ARIA landmarks and the skip-nav target. BEFORE: The layout renders <Sidebar> and a content div without role attributes, and the SkipToContent component has no matching target element with id='main-content'. AFTER: 1) Wrap the Sidebar component output in a <nav aria-label='Main navigation'> if not already done inside Sidebar itself. 2) Add id='main-content' and role='main' to the content area div that wraps the <Routes>. 3) Ensure the SkipToContent component links to '#main-content'. 4) Add aria-live='polite' to the <RouteAnnouncer> if not already present. 5) Add role='complementary' and aria-label='AI Assistant' to the CopilotPanel wrapper when it is rendered. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: MobileLayout component imported but mobile breakpoint detection may not gate sidebar rendering
issues.description: MobileLayout is imported and useMediaQuery is available, but the main App layout does not conditionally render a mobile bottom navigation or hide the desktop sidebar at mobile breakpoints. The hamburger Menu icon is imported but appears unused in the visible code, suggesting mobile navigation is incomplete.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, in the main layout rendering section: implement proper mobile/desktop layout switching. BEFORE: MobileLayout is imported but the layout always renders the desktop Sidebar regardless of screen width. The Menu icon from lucide-react is imported but unused. AFTER: 1) Use the existing useMediaQuery hook to detect mobile breakpoint (max-width: 768px). 2) When isMobile is true, render <MobileLayout> wrapping the routes instead of the desktop sidebar layout. 3) When isMobile is false, render the existing desktop layout with <Sidebar>. 4) Remove the unused Menu import if MobileLayout handles its own hamburger icon internally. 5) Ensure the mobile layout uses bottom tab navigation with touch targets of at least 44x44px per the design standards. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: createScopedClient proxy does not handle tables missing project_id column gracefully
issues.description: The UNSCOPED_TABLES set only contains 3 entries (organizations, organization_members, auth, storage) but many tables like audit_trail, notification_queue, weather_cache, and ai_conversations lack project_id or use different column names. Querying these through the scoped client appends an invalid .eq('project_id', ...) filter causing silent empty results.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/client.ts, in the UNSCOPED_TABLES Set definition: expand the set to include all tables that do not have a project_id column. BEFORE: UNSCOPED_TABLES only contains 'organizations', 'organization_members', 'auth', 'storage'. Tables like 'audit_trail', 'notification_queue', 'notification_preferences', 'user_phone', 'push_subscriptions', 'email_log', 'email_replies', 'email_templates', 'ai_conversations', 'ai_messages', 'calendar_oauth', 'procore_oauth', 'quickbooks_oauth', 'sage_intacct_oauth', 'weather_cache', 'profiles', 'project_members', 'portfolio_projects', and 'portfolios' either lack project_id or should not be auto-scoped. AFTER: Add these table names to the UNSCOPED_TABLES set: 'audit_trail', 'notification_queue', 'notification_preferences', 'user_phone', 'push_subscriptions', 'email_log', 'email_replies', 'email_templates', 'ai_conversations', 'ai_messages', 'calendar_oauth', 'procore_oauth', 'quickbooks_oauth', 'sage_intacct_oauth', 'weather_cache', 'profiles', 'project_members', 'portfolio_projects', 'portfolios'. This prevents silent empty results from invalid project_id filters on unscoped tables. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: LazyWithRetry wraps all pages but critical pages like Dashboard and RFIs should be prefetched on auth
issues.description: All pages use lazy loading with retry, but there is no route prefetching strategy. After login, the user waits for Dashboard chunk to download. The most visited pages (Dashboard, RFIs, DailyLog) should be prefetched immediately after authentication succeeds to eliminate perceived latency on first navigation.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, after the lazy import declarations: add a prefetch mechanism that starts downloading critical page chunks once the user is authenticated. BEFORE: All pages are lazy loaded on demand only. After login redirect to /dashboard, user sees the Suspense fallback while the Dashboard chunk downloads. AFTER: 1) Add a usePrefetchRoutes custom effect inside the main App component (or a dedicated hook). 2) When useAuth() returns an authenticated user, call the import functions for Dashboard, RFIs, and DailyLog without awaiting them: import('./pages/Dashboard'); import('./pages/RFIs'); import('./pages/DailyLog'). This triggers the browser to start fetching these chunks in parallel during idle time. 3) Wrap these prefetch calls in requestIdleCallback if available, with a setTimeout fallback of 1000ms to avoid blocking the initial render. 4) Add a comment explaining the prefetch strategy for future maintainers. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's API is a mature REST API with comprehensive documentation, versioning, and SDK support. SiteSync's v1 router is a good foundation but has implementation gaps (missing pathMatches) that would block any real integration consumer. Fieldwire's mobile-first approach includes proper responsive shell with bottom navigation, which SiteSync has imported but not wired. The scoped client pattern for multi-tenant isolation is architecturally sound and ahead of most competitors, but the incomplete UNSCOPED_TABLES list creates a data access risk that Procore avoids with explicit per-endpoint scoping.
summary: The Infrastructure & Routing module scores 56/100 with solid TypeScript architecture and a well-designed error handling hierarchy, but critical implementation gaps undermine reliability. The API v1 router has a missing pathMatches function that causes runtime errors on any route resolution. The scoped client's UNSCOPED_TABLES set is dangerously incomplete, causing silent empty results for 15+ tables. Mobile layout switching is imported but not wired, leaving mobile users with a desktop sidebar. Accessibility landmarks are absent from the app shell. Route prefetching after authentication would significantly improve perceived performance. Fixing the router and scoped client issues are the highest priority since they affect every downstream module.
